title: Retiring Bonds Before Maturity
event: Reyes Co. calls bonds with a face value of $500,000 and an unamortized discount of $20,000 (carrying value $480,000), paying the call price of 102 ($510,000). Prepare the retirement entry.

entries.label: Pay 510,000 to erase a 480,000 carrying value → 30,000 loss

lines.label: face off
lines.why: The face liability leaves the books.
lines.trap: Face on this line — the discount has its own line to clear.

lines.label: 510,000 − 480,000
lines.why: Paid 30,000 more than the debt's carrying value to escape it.
lines.trap: Measure against CARRYING VALUE (480,000), never face — comparing to face fakes a 10,000 loss.

lines.label: contra cleared
lines.why: The unamortized discount dies with the bonds.
lines.trap: Forgetting this line leaves a phantom contra-account attached to retired debt.

lines.label: call price 102
lines.why: 102% of face buys the bonds back.
lines.trap: Call prices quote as a percent of FACE: 1.02 × 500,000.

memorize.body: Gain/(loss) on retirement = carrying value − cash paid. CV = face − unamortized discount (or + premium).
Paying less than carrying value produces a GAIN — companies sometimes retire early precisely to capture it when rates move.

questions.prompt: Carrying value $480,000, called at 102 ($510,000). Loss on retirement = ?

distractors.feedback: You compared the 510,000 paid to FACE. The debt's book value is 480,000 — the loss is 30,000.
Early retirement at a price different from carrying value ALWAYS produces a recognized gain or loss.

build.scaffold: Face off, discount/premium off, cash out at the call price — the gain or loss is the plug vs carrying value.
